title: 12 Tiqimt — St. Constantine the Great & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "12 Tiqimt — St. Constantine the Great & The Assembly

narrative": "Tiqimt

St. Constantine the Great, Equal-to-the-Apostles (Monthly Commemoration)

Sts. Tarachus, Probus, and Andronicus, Martyrs

St. Domnina and Companions, Martyrs

The twelfth day of Tiqimt brings the monthly commemoration of St. Constantine the Great, and in the second month's interior light the figure of the first Christian emperor carries an aspect of his legacy that the brighter commemorations do not always foreground: the aspect of incompleteness, of a man who arrived at the threshold of the faith without quite crossing it in the ordinary way, who received baptism only at the very end of his life. Constantine died a catechumen receiving the waters — which is, from the interior life's perspective, a testimony about the length of the journey toward God. He was not a finished saint when his power was greatest; he was a man still approaching, still learning, still being drawn toward the font that awaited him at the end. In Tiqimt, Constantine's incompleteness is not a failure but an encouragement: the Covenant of Mercy holds the approach with the same faithfulness as the arrival.

Sts. Tarachus, Probus, and Andronicus were three men of different origins — a Roman soldier, a Pamphylian, and a Macedonian — who were tried together in Tarsus and Mopsuestia and eventually executed after a series of tortures conducted before large crowds. The accounts of their trial, which survive in early Christian literature, are remarkable for the quality of the conversation between the martyrs and their persecutors: not defiance or theological lecture but a kind of calm, searching dialogue in which the martyrs consistently demonstrate more clarity about what is happening than the imperial officials conducting the proceedings. Tarachus was an elderly man who had retired from military service; his age and his former service carried no weight before the tribunal, which is itself a testimony about the empire's ultimate values. In Tiqimt's assembly, these three present the interior life proved under interrogation — the peace that passes understanding made visible in the courtroom.

St. Domnina and her companions suffered in the Syrian tradition, a group of Christian women whose collective witness belongs to the great company of those who faced violence in community rather than in isolation. The communal dimension of their martyrdom is Tiqimt's particular contribution to the day's meditation: the interior life, however private its practice, is formed in community and tested in community, and the women who faced their persecution together demonstrate that the Covenant of Mercy is not only a covenant between the individual soul and God but a covenant that binds the community together in its most extreme conditions.

Constantine, Tarachus and his companions, and Domnina together on the twelfth day present three aspects of fidelity in its long approach and its extreme test. Constantine approaches across decades, never quite arriving until the final moment, and the mercy holds him in the approach. Tarachus and his companions arrive at the extreme test having prepared through a lifetime — the retired soldier, the provincial Christian, the Macedonian believer — and meet it with a composure that can only be the fruit of interiority. Domnina and her companions meet it in the solidarity of their shared life, each one sustained by the presence of the others. The Covenant of Mercy in Tiqimt is wide enough for all of them.
